name: Participant Inclusion Criteria Stringency
consequences: Tightening pre-admission screening (e.g., requiring ≥4 self-reported episodes per month and prior PSG confirmation of parasomnia activity) raises the probability of capturing events per participant but shrinks the recruitment pool and extends time-to-enrollment. Loosening criteria (e.g., accepting clinical suspicion with family report alone) accelerates recruitment and enrollment velocity but increases admissions of mimics or mild cases unlikely to produce captured events, consuming beds and staff annotation time without scientific yield.
options: Require documented parasomnia events on prior polysomnography or video recording before admission, accepting slower enrollment in exchange for near-certain event capture within 2–4 weeks

Accept clinical diagnosis with collateral bed-partner history as sufficient, prioritizing recruitment speed and participant diversity over guaranteed event yield, and plan for extended stays for borderline cases
Implement a two-tier admission pathway: fast-track entry for high-confidence cases (prior PSG evidence) and extended-monitoring tier for probable cases, with explicit resource budgets for each
review: Tightening criteria improves event yield but contradicts the goal of recruiting 50–70 participants within 3 years; loosening criteria fills beds faster but risks a scenario where the facility becomes a rehabilitation shelter rather than an event-capture engine. The options miss the deeper question: should the study prioritize Aim 2 (longitudinal phenotyping, which requires dense events) or Aim 1 (proving the facility is safe and operational, which tolerates lower event yield)? A third unexamined pathway is expanding stay duration for low-event participants rather than tightening pre-screening.

name: Enhanced PSG Scheduling Density
consequences: Conducting full PSG montage on every night yields maximum clinical-grade recording and ensures no event occurs without high-resolution capture, but consumes equipment time, technician annotation labor, and electrodes at costs that scale linearly with beds and occupancy. The tiered model (enhanced nights on entry, mid-stay, exit, plus escalation) trades maximum per-event resolution for operational efficiency and cost containment, accepting that some spontaneous events will be captured only via lower-resolution dry-electrode EEG.
options: Deploy full PSG every night across all 8 active suites, accepting doubled sensor-application burden and staff scheduling pressure in exchange for clinical-grade data on every potential event

Maintain the tiered protocol as designed (entry, mid-stay, exit, plus technician-flagged escalation), prioritizing cost control and technician workload sustainability over comprehensive resolution of every event

Implement a hybrid model where weeks 1–2 receive nightly PSG for all participants to establish baseline arousal and movement patterns, then step down to tiered PSG thereafter, balancing early-stage data density with later-stage cost efficiency
review: The core tension is clinical rigor versus operational sustainability: full PSG every night is scientifically conservative but risks technician burnout and may exhaust the €450K equipment budget before year two. The current tiered model assumes technician flagging is reliable, but if borderline events are frequently missed in low-resolution streams, Aim 3 (event-triage tool benchmarking) becomes biased. The options do not address a critical unknown: what is the false-negative rate of dry-electrode EEG for the parasomnias under study, and how does that error rate propagate into algorithm training and validation?

name: Staffing Model for Multi-Suite Coverage
consequences: Three technicians on rotating night shifts provide safe 8-suite coverage with margin for event response and annotation, but require all three to be cross-trained to clinical competency and willing to work overnight, creating recruitment and retention risk in a competitive academic labor market. Expanding staffing to four technicians per night reduces fatigue and improves annotation throughput but adds €200–250K annually to personnel costs; conversely, lean staffing of two technicians per night saves costs but creates safety bottlenecks if both are managing simultaneous events or participants in distress.
options: Hire four full-time night technicians staggered to provide two-per-night coverage with one overlap hour, explicitly budgeting the additional €250K annually and building technician retention incentives (shift premiums, professional development in sleep medicine, career pathways) into the staffing plan

Maintain the three-technician rotating model as budgeted, with explicit protocol for when occupancy exceeds 6 active suites simultaneously—triggering either temporary admission pauses or on-call senior staffing at overtime rates

Adopt a hybrid: two full-time night technicians as the baseline, supplemented by a resident physician or senior graduate student from the affiliated epileptology department on rotating on-call status, sharing the night-shift burden and creating clinical training synergy
review: The three-technician design assumes perfect scheduling and no illness or attrition; in reality, a single technician's departure during year one could undermine the safe-operations narrative required for Aim 1 validation. The four-technician option is fiscally conservative relative to the €3.8M budget (only 6–7% more) but may be harder to recruit in a Bonn labor market. The hybrid option exploits a resource (epileptology resident training) that is mentioned but not formally budgeted, potentially creating a hidden constraint if the residency program cannot reliably provide coverage.

name: Temporal Scope and Stay Duration Flexibility
consequences: A fixed 2-week minimum and 10-week ceiling on stay duration provides clarity for participant scheduling and frees up beds after a predictable interval, but a participant with zero events after 8 weeks consumes a bed slot without contribution to Aim 2 or Aim 3. Extending stays for zero-event participants (as currently allowed up to 10 weeks) increases the probability of capturing at least one event but delays turnover and may lock low-yield participants into protracted stays that become both burdensome and unproductive, creating ethical tension around continued enrollment without scientific return.
options: Enforce a hard 4-week stay ceiling for all participants, prioritizing bed turnover and recruitment momentum over extended event-yield hunting, and redirect zero-event participants to a separate outpatient actigraphy follow-up protocol

Allow flexible 2–10 week stays with explicit mid-point review (day 14): if zero events captured by day 14, jointly decide with the participant whether to extend (up to 10 weeks total) or exit, with priority extensions given to high-confidence cases who are willing to continue

Implement a dynamic stay-length algorithm: participants with ≥2 captured events receive standard 4-week discharges; those with 1 event are offered a 2-week extension contingent on event rate; those with zero events at week 4 are transitioned to home-based actigraphy monitoring with twice-weekly check-ins, freeing the residential bed
review: Shorter stays increase bed throughput and reach the 50–70 enrollment target faster, but reduce per-participant event yield and undermine Aim 2 (longitudinal characterization requires within-person temporal clustering analysis, which needs 4+ weeks minimum per participant). Longer stays improve event capture but consume finite residential capacity and risk participant dropout due to prolonged institutional stay. The real tension is unaddressed: whether Aim 2 requires sufficient events per person to analyze temporal clustering at all, or whether the study can be statistically powered with rare events pooled across 50+ participants without deep individual-level within-person dynamics.

name: Event-Triage Algorithm Development Pathway
consequences: Building a semi-automated triage tool (Aim 3) using rule-based logic (e.g., thresholds on movement amplitude, arousal duration, muscle atonia patterns) is interpretable and can be deployed early but requires extensive hand-tuning and may miss variant phenotypes. Machine-learning approaches (trained on dual-scored gold-standard events) are more flexible but require 100+ labeled events minimum, longer development timelines, and risk overfitting to early-study participant characteristics if the training set is small or skewed. Outsourcing algorithm development to a commercial vendor accelerates timeline but surrenders control over model transparency and may introduce licensing constraints.
options: Develop rule-based event-detection heuristics using published AASM morphology criteria and custom thresholds derived from the first 20 dual-scored events, prioritizing transparency and early deployment even at the cost of modest recall rates (sensitivity ~70–75%)

Train a gradient-boosted or neural-network classifier on events captured during year one and early year two (target: 80–150 gold-standard labeled events), accepting 6-month development latency but targeting higher sensitivity (80–85%) and generalization to variant phenotypes

Commission a commercial polysomnography software vendor to adapt their existing seizure-detection or arousal-detection algorithm to parasomnia event classification, trading away model transparency and publication control in exchange for a pre-built, clinically mature pipeline
review: Rule-based tools are simple but may anchor to AASM criteria that were developed for symptomatic single-night PSG, not continuous residential monitoring where event morphology varies with sleep stage saturation and recovery dynamics. Machine-learning approaches require sufficient labeled data, but the project may fall short of the 80–150 events target if participant recruitment or event yield underperforms—leaving the algorithm with a weak training foundation. The outsourcing option avoids this risk but creates a critical vulnerability: if the vendor's algorithm performs poorly on NREM parasomnias (outside their primary seizure or apnea use case), the entire Aim 3 timeline derails, and the team has less institutional leverage to iterate. None of the options acknowledge that Aim 3 explicitly states the goal is benchmarking against 'dual human scorer agreement, not promising autonomous diagnosis,' which suggests the triage tool may never be held to clinical deployment standards, reframing its purpose as annotation-efficiency aid rather than diagnostic device.

name: Physical Capacity Expansion Timing
consequences: The facility launches with 8 active suites (of 12 available) and defers expansion until pilot data prove operational soundness, cost-efficiency, and acceptable staffing ratios. Expanding to 12 suites by month 12 (if early metrics are favorable) increases enrollment throughput and event capture by 50%, accelerating the path to 50–70 participants, but requires immediate recruitment and training of 1–2 additional night technicians and raises facility noise and fire-safety coordination complexity. Delaying expansion until month 24 (end of year two) reduces operational risk but leaves 4 suites dark and may cause the recruitment and ethics board to question why facility capacity is underutilized.
options: Commit to expansion to 10 active suites by month 12 if pilot metrics (safety events, event-capture rate, staffing burden score) meet prespecified thresholds by month 6, with contingency funding (€100–150K) already earmarked in the year-one budget for additional technician hiring

Defer all expansion decisions until month 18, requiring a formal go/no-go review of operational data, event yield, and participant feedback; if expansion proceeds, target 11–12 suites by month 24 with a corresponding staffing and budget adjustment

Immediately lease or renovate all 12 suites by month 6 (within year one), committing to the full facility footprint upfront and hiring 4 additional technician positions (2 per night shift), betting that scale and operational rhythm stabilize faster in a fully activated environment than in a phased rollout
review: Phased expansion (option 1) aligns with the stated risk-mitigation logic—prove the model before scaling—but risks appearing timid if early results are strong, and month-12 technician hiring is a compressed timeline in the German academic labor market. Full expansion (option 3) accelerates enrollment but violates the stated deferral strategy and doubles the budget risk if operational soundness is not yet established. The core tension is unspoken: does the 3-year timeline assume 8 or 12 suites for the success criteria (50–70 participants)? If the math was done for 8-suite occupancy, expansion becomes a bonus; if the timeline covertly assumes 12 suites, option 1 is the only realistic path, and the 'defer' language is misleading.

name: Data Sharing and Secondary Research Access Policy
consequences: Restricting public data sharing to de-identified physiology and sensors (excluding video) maximizes participant privacy and ethics-board compliance but limits downstream research impact—future meta-analyses or algorithm developers cannot access rich behavioral context that video would provide. Opening a carefully curated video-access pathway (e.g., researcher accounts with institutional review and data-use agreements) dramatically increases research value but creates complex governance and persistent privacy exposure, particularly if de-identification of video is incomplete or re-identification attacks succeed over time. Depositing only summary statistics (not raw time series) accelerates publication and reduces privacy burden but sacrifices the reproducibility and algorithm-training potential that raw data enables.
options: Deposit raw de-identified EEG, sensor, and behavioral time-series data in a recognized repository (e.g., OpenNeuro, PhysioNet) with public access under CC-BY or equivalent license, and distribute video data only through a restricted-access institutional portal with individual researcher agreements and explicit ethics-board oversight for each request

Publish all results as de-identified summary statistics and aggregated phenotyping findings in peer-reviewed journals and deposit only annotated event clips (30–60 second excerpts centered on detected events, with faces and identifying context blurred) in an open repository, retaining full video in institutional secure storage for 10 years for internal validation and replication studies

Create a tiered-access ecosystem: raw EEG and sensor data fully open on OpenNeuro; video data and detailed behavioral annotations available through a formal Secondary Research Committee (oversight by the SAB) that reviews and approves external researcher proposals; pharmaceutical industry partners granted expedited access to de-identified data and non-proprietary algorithms under the existing pre-publication window agreement
review: Full open access to physiology data is scientifically generous and accelerates knowledge dissemination but may underestimate the privacy risk from combining time-stamped behavioral data (sleep/wake cycles, arousal patterns) with demographic metadata in external hands over a 10-year horizon. The video-only restriction minimizes re-identification risk but wastes the richest behavioral signal and is likely to disappoint potential collaborators seeking to develop cross-modal algorithms. The tiered-access model introduces governance overhead—the SAB was not budgeted for Secondary Research Committee work—and creates the appearance of scientific gatekeeping if access is slow or rejections occur. A pragmatic weakness across all options: the plan does not specify how long raw data will be retained post-publication (10 years, indefinite?), which affects both privacy exposure duration and cost of long-term storage.

name: Funding Risk and Contingency Activation
consequences: The budget is gated on DFG grant success and conditional year-three funding (€800K) is contingent on month-24 progress. If DFG funding is delayed or reduced, the facility cannot renovate or hire staff on schedule, deferring launch by 6–12 months and cascading into late recruitment and shortened operational window. Securing pharmaceutical industry partnership (currently optional upside) injects €300–500K annually but introduces publication timelines and potential reputational risk if trial results are seen as industry-influenced; conversely, declining industry funding to preserve full academic independence may force a hard choice at month 18 if DFG is insufficient or enrollment lags.
options: Pursue DFG and University of Bonn funding as the sole financial foundation, with no pharmaceutical industry partnership, and establish a hard month-12 go/no-go trigger: if cumulative funding received (DFG + university) is <€1.5M by month 12, pause expansion and reduce participant intake to 15–20 per year until funding stabilizes or alternative sources materialize

Negotiate and finalize a pharmaceutical partnership agreement in parallel with DFG submission (target: signed MOU by month 3), securing €350–400K annually in exchange for data access and the 30-day pre-publication review window, treating this as the contingency floor below which year-two and year-three operations remain viable even if DFG is partially delayed

Structure funding as a hybrid tier: DFG as the primary academic grant; university internal funding as the operational baseline sufficient for year one at minimal scale (5–6 suites, reduced staffing); and pharma partnership as an optional accelerant that, if signed by month 6, enables full 8-suite launch and on-time year-two scaling, with public disclosure of all funding sources and oversight measures in all publications
review: Relying exclusively on DFG leaves the facility vulnerable to the German research funding cycle (6–9 month decisions, review delays); a year-long wait for award notification translates to March 2027 project start, compressing the 3-year timeline to 2 years of operations before the month-36 evaluation. Pursuing pharma funding reduces financial risk but creates potential bias narratives, particularly if results align with sponsor interests in any way. The tiered option (option 3) is pragmatic but requires transparency disciplines that add governance overhead—every publication, every presentation must disclose funding sources—and the SAB may face pressure to perceive conflicts even if none exist. The deeper risk unaddressed: the budget assumes 50–70 enrollment targets, but if recruitment falls short (common in early longitudinal sleep studies), the per-participant cost climbs, and contingency reserves erode; no option includes explicit contingency de-scoping (e.g., reducing success criteria from 70 to 50 participants) if funding constraints demand it.

name: Pre-Admission Clinical Screening Rigor and Adjudication Authority
consequences: Stringent screening (sleep medicine specialist interview, bed-partner collateral, prior PSG review, and formal exclusion checklist) reduces admissions of participants with undiagnosed epilepsy, severe untreated sleep apnea, or injury risk—protecting participant safety and avoiding event misclassification. However, rigorous gatekeeping delays enrollment, risks losing referrals from regional neurologists unfamiliar with the checklist, and may systematically exclude borderline or complex cases that could inform phenotyping work.
options: Implement a multi-tiered screening protocol with PI-led adjudication of all borderline cases, supplemented by a remote pre-visit video interview with the sleep medicine specialist to confirm clinical history before scheduling—escalating uncertainty to a single decision-maker rather than distributing it.

Establish a streamlined phone-screen by the study coordinator followed by immediate referral-back to the referring clinician if any exclusion flag emerges, reducing administrative friction but accepting that some referred participants will be turned away without direct PI involvement.

Create a parallel fast-track pathway for referrals from University Hospital Bonn's own sleep clinic using their existing clinical documentation, with out-of-network referrals subject to full screening and a 2-week wait, reducing delays for high-confidence local cases while maintaining rigor for external sources.
review: The tension is between safety (strict exclusion prevents high-risk admissions) and recruitment velocity (strict screening lengthens delays and frustrates referring clinicians). The options miss a key weakness: none address how the PI will scale adjudication authority once enrollment ramps to 30+ participants per year—either the PI becomes a bottleneck or decision-making delegated to less experienced staff risks safety drift.

name: Enhanced Night PSG Sampling Frequency and Timing Strategy
consequences: Conducting full scalp-EEG polysomnography on nights one, mid-stay, and final-night captures acute adaptation effects, mid-stay baseline stability, and post-habituation behavior in a time-efficient manner. Limiting high-resolution captures to three nights per stay preserves budget and technician time for 30+ participants yearly, but risks missing rare sleep cycle-dependent events and cannot characterize intra-stay temporal clustering if episodes cluster outside the scheduled window.
options: Assign enhanced-night PSG on a fixed schedule (arrival, day 14, departure) regardless of event activity, treating the three nights as a standardized sampling frame for all participants to ensure comparable data architecture and comparable detection opportunity across the cohort.

Deploy enhanced PSG adaptively: baseline night one plus mid-stay plus final night, but also trigger an unscheduled escalation night within 24 hours of any technician-flagged event to capture recurrence physiology at clinical resolution, accepting variable operational load and reduced predictability for staffing.

Randomize the mid-stay enhanced night across days 7–21 for each participant using a pre-generated schedule, balancing the goal of uncorrelated sampling with the administrative overhead of communicating variable timing to participants and technicians, and reducing the risk of a systematic temporal bias if events cluster on particular days of stay.
review: The core tension is between standardization (same three nights allows uniform analysis) and responsiveness (triggering on events captures high-value recurrence physiology). The weakness: none of the options addresses whether the enhanced-night schedule should be disclosed to participants in advance—blinded scheduling could suppress anticipatory anxiety but introduces logistical friction for consent and participant communication.

name: Inter-Rater Reliability Scoring Infrastructure and Escalation Protocol
consequences: Requiring two independent human raters to score all overnight sessions using AASM criteria and reporting inter-rater reliability establishes the episodic ground truth against which event-triage algorithms will be benchmarked, protecting the validity of Aim 3. However, independent dual-scoring doubles the annotation workload; if agreement falls below acceptable thresholds, resolving disagreements requires a tiebreaker mechanism, and if disagreement is systematic, the protocol must decide whether to revise criteria or exclude data.
options: Employ two full-time scoring specialists with formal sleep medicine technician certification, each scoring all sessions independently, and establish a documented third-rater arbitration process (PI review) only for discrepancies exceeding 5% of flagged events, prioritizing inter-rater reliability metrics over annotation throughput.

Outsource scoring to a commercial sleep-lab service or university-affiliated scoring consortium with established AASM scoring pipelines, bundling dual-rater agreements into the contracted deliverable and accepting less direct control over tiebreaker rules but reducing on-site staffing burden and leveraging external standardization.

Train one core in-house scorer to AASM standards while assigning a rotating subset of sessions (30% of all nights, stratified by participant and sleep stage) to a second external scorer, reducing dual-scoring costs while accepting greater measurement variance and limiting inter-rater reliability evidence to the subset.
review: The tension is between scoring accuracy (dual independent raters with arbitration) and operational cost (doubling annotation labor). The weakness: none of the options specifies how to handle systematic disagreement—for example, if one rater consistently flags arousals as parasomnia-related and the other does not, the protocol needs a pre-defined escalation (retraining, criteria revision, or expert consensus calibration) rather than simply averaging percentages.

name: Incident Response Staffing Model and Technician Multi-Tasking Boundaries
consequences: Assigning three technicians rotating night shifts to cover 8 active suites provides a nominal 2.7 suites per technician, but allows one technician to respond to a flagged event (assessment, participant reassurance, escalation PSG setup) while others maintain monitor coverage. Expanding to 12 suites without adding technicians erodes safety margins; staffing models that require technicians to simultaneously annotate, monitor, and respond during events risk missing secondary incidents or producing incomplete real-time scoring.
options: Maintain a hard rule of one technician per 2–3 suites maximum, adding a fourth rotating technician before expanding beyond 10 active suites and accepting the personnel cost increase as a prerequisite for scaling rather than deferring it until failure occurs.

Implement a tiered incident-response protocol where a technician's primary duty is always real-time monitoring and incident response, with a separate part-time scoring technician (e.g., day shift or weekend backfill) assigned to all annotation and algorithm testing, decoupling response and annotation labor.

Assign one designated 'event-response technician' per night (a fourth person) whose sole role is incident management, technician support, and equipment troubleshooting, while the rotating three technicians focus on suite monitoring and real-time annotation, accepting the highest staffing cost but removing contention for attention during critical moments.
review: The tension is between coverage adequacy (more technicians increase safety margins) and staffing cost (9-person team already substantial; each additional hire adds roughly €80K+ yearly). The weakness: none of the options addresses cross-training depth or what happens when a technician calls in sick—a single-night absence could reduce coverage below safe ratios if no on-call backup exists.

name: Expansion Readiness Criteria and Capacity Decision Gating
consequences: Deferring physical expansion from 8 to 12 suites until operational proof (safety, data quality, false-alarm rates, staffing resilience) is demonstrated protects against over-committing renovation budget and staffing to an untested model. However, delaying expansion until month 18–24 risks losing enrolled momentum if word-of-mouth recruitment builds demand, and forces 35+ participants per year into 8 suites, extending average stay duration and reducing annual event capture if participants must queue.
options: Set explicit expansion gates: by month 12, if all-cause technician safety incidents are zero, inter-rater agreement exceeds 85%, and waiting-list size exceeds 10 participants for more than 4 weeks, proceed immediately with phase-two renovation; if any gate fails, defer expansion review to month 18.

Commit to renovating four additional suites in parallel with year-one operations on a fixed timeline (summer 2027), regardless of pilot outcomes, accepting the construction risk and assuming that operational lessons will be incorporated during build-out rather than waiting for pilot completion.

Establish a rolling assessment every quarter: if enrollment velocity reaches 12+ new admissions per quarter and captured-event yield exceeds 0.8 events per participant, trigger a phased expansion planning process (architect, contractor selection) without committing final sign-off until month 18 operational review, allowing faster action if conditions warrant.
review: The tension is between cautious validation (don't expand until proven safe and effective) and market responsiveness (build capacity if demand justifies it). The weakness: none of the options defines what 'acceptable data quality' means quantitatively—does it mean a minimum signal-to-noise ratio in EEG, a minimum event-capture rate, or absence of systematic sensor drift? Without pre-defined thresholds, the expansion decision may become subjective or delayed indefinitely.

name: Event-Triage Algorithm Development Scope and Validation Stringency
consequences: Developing semi-automated tools that rank probable episodes and reduce manual review burden against dual human scorer agreement is a realistic Aim 3 goal for early-stage validation—the algorithm is evaluated as a triage filter, not a diagnostic instrument. However, committing to 'semi-automated' leaves open what features the algorithm uses (raw sensor fusion, hand-engineered feature extraction, or black-box learned representations), how sensitivity-specificity trade-offs are reported, and whether the model will be repositioned as a deployable clinical tool before the 3-year study ends.
options: Restrict algorithm development to rule-based heuristics and hand-engineered features (e.g., mattress pressure changes, head-mounted accelerometer thresholds, EMG amplitude) that are fully interpretable and documented in the methods section, foregoing higher accuracy for explainability and ensuring the triage tool remains a research instrument with clear limitations.

Allow supervised learning methods (e.g., random forest, logistic regression) trained on the dual-scored event labels with structured cross-validation and sensitivity-specificity reporting, but prohibit any deployment, marketing, or claims of clinical utility outside the 3-year study period without explicit external validation and ethics review.

Develop both a simple heuristic baseline and a machine-learning alternative in parallel, reporting agreement and discrepancies between the two approaches, using this comparison to ground where algorithm predictions diverge from human judgment and what features drive the disagreement—prioritizing transparency over single-algorithm performance.
review: The tension is between algorithmic sophistication (more complex models may achieve higher sensitivity) and interpretability and external validity (black-box models trained on a single site's 50–70 participants are not generalizable). The weakness: none of the options prohibits the team from pre-registering the algorithm development plan or committing to a prospective hold-out test set—retroactively choosing the final model based on what performs best on the study data introduces selection bias and inflates reported performance.

name: De-Identified Data Sharing Repository and Access Control Model
consequences: Depositing de-identified physiological sensor data and PSG signals in a public repository (e.g., OpenNeuro, Zenodo) with clear data use agreements maximizes secondary research reuse and supports reproducible science. Video recording and behavioral annotations carry severe privacy constraints and will not be publicly shared, but restricting access to raw sensor streams also limits external method development and replication studies—deciding the granularity of de-identification (e.g., how much temporal or demographic information is safe to release) requires balancing reusability with residual re-identification risk.
options: Deposit all time-synchronized sensor data (EEG, EOG, EMG, mattress pressure, accelerometry, ambient sound spectrogram, temperature) with coarse demographic bins (age decade, binary sex, parasomnia type) and no circadian time stamps on each signal, minimizing re-identification risk but reducing the utility of longitudinal within-person analyses for external researchers.

Release two-tiered access: raw sensor streams with fine temporal resolution and participant identifiers available only through a formal data use agreement and institutional review process (to external academic researchers), while a coarser annotated dataset with event labels and summary statistics is openly available without credentials, allowing broad method-sharing while restricting raw data to vetted users.

Publish only the scored event labels, event-triage algorithm predictions, and aggregated statistics (e.g., event frequency, episode duration, temporal clustering patterns) in supplementary tables and code repositories, withholding the raw physiological streams entirely to eliminate re-identification concern but accepting that the dataset cannot serve as a resource for future sensor-fusion or algorithm-development studies.
review: The tension is between open science (public data maximizes reuse and accelerates the field) and privacy protection (even de-identified sensor sequences can be re-identified if correlated with external metadata). The weakness: none of the options addresses what happens if a future researcher or bad actor attempts re-identification attacks on released data—the deposit decision is made once, and secondary harm is hard to retroactively address; the protocol should specify how to respond to re-identification risks discovered post-publication.
